title: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukaemia
oneLiner: A mature B-cell neoplasm of elderly causing clonal lymphocytosis, painless lymphadenopathy, and immune dysfunction; it evolves slowly but can turn aggressive.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:**
A 72-year-old retired factory worker attends his GP for a routine blood test. He feels “a bit tired lately” but is otherwise well. Examination reveals rubbery, mobile lymph nodes in the neck and axillae, no fever. Full blood count shows a white cell count of 28,000/µL with >70% small lymphocytes. The lymphocytes look mature with scattered “smudge” cells on the film. He is diagnosed with CLL but has no anaemia, thrombocytopenia, or hepatosplenomegaly — Rai stage 0.

**Dissect the vignette (pattern clues):**
| Clue | What it tells you |
|------|-------------------|
| Age >60 | CLL is a disease of the elderly; median age 72. |
| Incidental finding | Most CLL is asymptomatic at diagnosis — a classic “lab surprise.” |
| Rubbery, non-tender nodes | CLL nodes are typically firm, mobile, painless; contrast with infection (tender) or lymphoma (larger, conglomerate). |
| Lymphocytosis >10,000/µL + mature small cells | Diagnostic threshold for CLL is B-cell count ≥5000/µL (often much higher). |
| Smudge cells (basket cells) | Fragile CLL lymphocytes crush on smear — almost pathognomonic. |
| No cytopenias | Early stage (Rai 0) — management is watch-and-wait. |

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
- **“Recurrent infections”** – A 70-year-old with multiple pneumonias, low immunoglobulins, but only mild lymphocytosis (10k). CLL can present with hypogammaglobulinemia as the dominant feature.
- **“Autoimmune haemolytic anaemia”** – A patient with jaundice, positive DAT, and a moderate lymphocytosis; AIHA can be the first manifestation of CLL, leading to a diagnostic detour.
- **“Painful node, fever, weight loss”** – This suggests Richter transformation (aggressive lymphoma arising from CLL) — a late event, but it can be the presenting scenario in a previously undiagnosed patient.

---
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition:**
CLL is a cancer of mature B-lymphocytes that accumulate in the blood, bone marrow, and lymphoid organs. These cells live too long (defective apoptosis) and crowd out normal haematopoiesis, but they function poorly – leading to both cytopenias and immunodeficiency.

**Why it matters clinically:**
- **Frequency:** CLL is the most common leukaemia in adults in Western countries (incidence ~5/100,000), with a long natural history that most clinicians will encounter.
- **Stakes:** Although often indolent (many die *with* it, not *of* it), it can transform into aggressive lymphoma (Richter) or cause life-threatening cytopenias and infections. “Watch and wait” is a real management strategy — doing nothing is often correct.
- **Common pitfalls:** Over-treating early-stage disease, missing Richter transformation, misinterpreting smudge cells, and forgetting CLL as a cause of autoimmune cytopenias.

---
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind Th:
**The story of development:**
CLL arises from a single B-cell clone that has undergone antigen activation (likely from a self- or microbial antigen) but fails to undergo apoptosis. The key abnormality is **overexpression of BCL2** (an anti-apoptotic protein), often due to loss of microRNA regulators (miR-15a/miR-16-1 on chromosome 13q14). This gives CLL cells a survival advantage, not a proliferation advantage — they accumulate slowly over years.

**Why each key sign occurs (mechanism → sign):**
- **Lymphocytosis:** The clone expands relentlessly, crowding the blood. The cells are CD5+, CD23+, surface Ig weak – this immunophenotype distinguishes CLL from mantle cell (CD5+, but CD23-) and other lymphomas.
- **Lymphadenopathy & splenomegaly:** CLL cells home to lymphoid follicles and interact with the microenvironment (nurse-like cells, T-cells) that provide survival signals. Nodes enlarge painlessly.
- **Hypogammaglobulinemia & repeated infections:** The malignant B-cells suppress normal B-cell development (via cytokines and competition) leading to low IgG, impaired antibody responses, and defective opsonisation – especially encapsulated bacteria (pneumococcus, haemophilus).
- **Cytopenias (anaemia, thrombocytopenia) – direct:** Bone marrow infiltration by CLL cells replaces normal haematopoietic tissue. This is a late sign (stage III/IV).
- **Cytopenias – immune-mediated:** CLL disrupts T-cell regulation, leading to autoantibodies against RBCs (AIHA – warm Coombs positive) or platelets (ITP). This can occur even with mild marrow involvement.
- **Smudge cells:** The CLL lymphocyte cell membrane is extremely fragile due to abnormal cytoskeleton – simply making the blood film crushes them. Not just an artefact; it reflects the defective biology.

**Connect to pattern recognition:**
The incidental lymphocytosis in our vignette is pure accumulation. The absence of cytopenias means the bone marrow reserve is still adequate. The smudge cells are your clue that these aren’t reactive lymphocytes (which are robust). The rubbery nodes are tactile evidence of clonal B-cell homing.

---
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Cause / Trigger | 1-line Stem Clue |
|----------|----------------|------------------|
| **Idiopathic** (vast majority) | Spontaneous clonal expansion; no known initiator. | An elderly patient with no toxin exposure, just “found” on routine labs. |
| **Familial / Genetic** | Rare; first-degree relative of CLL patient has 2-6× risk. Associated with certain HLA haplotypes. | Ask: “Anyone in the family had leukaemia or lymphoma?” |
| **Environmental** | Agent Orange (Vietnam vet), benzene, radiation – weak or inconsistent associations. | “Worked with solvents” or “service in Vietnam.” |
| **Precursor lesion** | Monoclonal B-cell lymphocytosis (MBL) – a pre-malignant state with <5000 B-cells/µL. | Incidental finding; progression to CLL at ~1–2% per year. |
| **Triggers for progression** | TET2, NOTCH1, or SF3B1 mutations; del17p (p53 loss); unmutated IGHV. | “Patient who was stable now has rapidly rising WCC, B symptoms, new cytopenias.” |

**Most common cause:** Idiopathic (no clear trigger). CLL is a disease of biological ageing of the immune system, not a reaction to a specific vaccine, virus, or toxin.

---
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria (IWCLL 2018):**
- **Clonal B-cell count ≥5000/µL** in peripheral blood for at least 3 months.
- If B-cells <5000/µL but with cytopenia or symptoms, bone marrow biopsy may be needed.

**Test-by-test approach:**

| Test | What it shows | Why you order it | How to interpret |
|------|---------------|-----------------|------------------|
| **FBC + blood film** | Lymphocytosis, smudge cells | First step – screens for CLL | Lymphocytes >5000/µL, mature, smudge cells present. If blasts or prolymphocytes >55%, think PLL. |
| **Immunophenotyping (flow cytometry)** | Surface markers: CD5+, CD23+, CD20 (dim), sIg (weak) | Confirms clonal B-cell and distinguishes from other lymphomas | **CD5+ and CD23+ is the hallmark.** Mantle cell: CD5+, CD23-. Splenic marginal zone: CD5-, CD23-. |
| **Cytogenetics (FISH)** | del13q, del11q, del17p, trisomy 12 | Prognostic stratification; guides therapy | **del17p** = p53 loss, poor response to chemo; fits for targeted therapy (BTK inhibitors). **del11q** = ATM loss, bulky disease, also poor. **Isolated del13q** = good prognosis. |
| **IGHV mutational status** | Mutated vs unmutated IGHV genes | Predicts time to progression | **Mutated** (~50%) → indolent course, median survival >20 years. **Unmutated** → aggressive, rapid progression, need early treatment. |
| **Serum immunoglobulins** | IgG, IgA, IgM | Baseline for infection risk | Low IgG → consider IVIG if recurrent infections. |
| **Direct antiglobulin test (DAT)** | Coombs test for AIHA | Check for autoimmune haemolysis | Positive → supports treatment with steroids; avoid fludarabine-based regimens (may exacerbate AIHA). |
| **Bone marrow biopsy** | Percentage of lymphocytes in marrow | Reserved for cytopenias of unclear cause, or before initiating therapy (document baseline) | >30% lymphocytes is typical; but not required for diagnosis if blood criteria met. |

**Single finding that confirms:** Flow cytometry showing a clonal B-cell population that coexpresses **CD5 and CD23** in the context of an absolute B-cell count ≥5000/µL.

**Single finding that rules out:** Absence of a clonal B-cell excess (B-cells <5000/µL) and no other clinical features – but watch for MBL.

---
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Philosophy:** CLL is often incurable with conventional therapies, but can be managed for years. Treatment is initiated **only for symptomatic or progressive disease** (Rai stage I–IV with B symptoms, symptomatic lymphadenopathy, severe cytopenias, rapid doubling time). Staging systems: Rai (0–IV) or Binet (A–C).

**Immediate (first hour / first visit):**
- If asymptomatic, **no intervention** – explain the concept of “watch and wait.” Reassure the patient that doing nothing is evidence-based.
- If symptomatic (e.g., painful nodes, night sweats, fatigue from anaemia) → assess urgent need for treatment, but don’t start chemotherapy in the first hour. Order confirmatory tests.
- Obtain blood for immunophenotyping, FISH, IGHV, LDH, creatinine, LFTs, serum immunoglobulins, DAT.
- **What NOT to do:** Do not start steroids or chemotherapy without a definitive diagnosis and staging.

**Ward / clinic (24–72 hours) – starting treatment (first-line):**
Choice depends on: fitness (age, comorbidities), IGHV status, del17p/del11q.

- **Fit patient, no del17p, mutated IGHV:**
  - *FCR* (fludarabine 25mg/m² IV day 1–3 + cyclophosphamide 250mg/m² IV day 1–3 + rituximab 375mg/m² IV day 1, cycle 28 days) x 6 cycles.
  - **Why:** Fludarabine-based chemoimmunotherapy is the historic standard; high CR rates in mutated IGHV.
  - **Pitfall:** Avoid fludarabine in patients with AIHA (may worsen haemolysis). Use bendamustine + rituximab (BR) instead.

- **Unfit patient, no del17p:**
  - *BR* (bendamustine 90mg/m² IV day 1–2 + rituximab 375mg/m² day 1, cycle 28 days) x 6 cycles.
  - **Why:** Less myelosuppressive than FCR, better tolerated in elderly.

- **Patient with del17p or high-risk (NOTCH1, TP53):**
  - *Ibrutinib* 420mg PO daily (BTK inhibitor) continuously until progression.
  - **Why:** Bypasses p53 pathway; excellent response even in TP53-mutated.
  - Alternative: *Venetoclax* (BCL2 inhibitor) + obinutuzumab – fixed duration (12 cycles) but requires TLS prophylaxis.

**Long-term:**
- Monitor with FBC every 3–6 months if on active therapy. Watch for drug toxicities: ibrutinib → atrial fibrillation, bleeding, HTN; venetoclax → TLS (monitor potassium, uric acid, phosphate; hydrate, give allopurinol).
- If disease progression on ibrutinib → switch to venetoclax (or vice versa). Consider allogeneic stem cell transplant for younger fit patients with refractory disease.
- **Supportive care:**
  - Vaccinate against encapsulated bacteria (pneumococcal, haemophilus, meningococcal) – **avoid live vaccines** (CLL is immunocompromised).
  - IVIG 400mg/kg every 3–4 weeks if IgG <500 mg/dL and recurrent infections.
  - Growth factors (G-CSF) for neutropenia.
- **What NOT to do:** Don’t use rituximab alone (poor activity). Don’t use maintenance chlorambucil (outdated). Don’t treat asymptomatic early-stage disease – it has no survival benefit.

---
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | CLL | Mantle Cell Lymphoma (MCL) | Hairy Cell Leukaemia (HCL) | Prolymphocytic Leukaemia (PLL) |
|---------|-----|----------------------------|----------------------------|--------------------------------|
| **Pain** | No pain from nodes | Nodes may be bulky but painless | Splenic pain (infarction) | Splenic pain, bone pain |
| **Exam** | Rubbery cervical/axillary nodes, mild splenomegaly | Rapidly growing nodes, often Waldeyer’s ring involvement | Macrosplenomegaly, pancytopenia; **no lymphadenopathy** | Massive splenomegaly, minimal nodes |
| **Blood film** | Small lymphocytes, smudge cells | Small lymphocytes with irregular nuclei; **no smudge cells** | Lymphocytes with cytoplasmic “hairs” (ruffled borders) | Large lymphocytes with prominent nucleoli; often >55% prolymphocytes |
| **Flow cytometry** | CD5+, CD23+, weak sIg | CD5+, CD23–, strong sIg (cyclin D1+) | CD5–, CD103+, CD25+ (tartrate-resistant acid phosphatase +) | CD5+ (in B-PLL) or CD5–; CD23–; strong sIg |
| **Key differentiator** | **CD5+ and CD23+** (classic) | **Cyclin D1 overexpression** (t(11;14)) | **Splenomegaly + pancytopenia + “hairy” cells** | **>55% prolymphocytes**; aggressive course |

---
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
| Complication | How it presents | Key signs | What to do |
|--------------|----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Richter transformation** (diffuse large B-cell lymphoma) | Rapidly enlarging node(s), fever, night sweats, weight loss, rising LDH. | PET-positive node (SUV >10); biopsy shows large B-cells. | Urgent haematology review; treat with R-CHOP or DA-EPOCH; consider transplant. |
| **Autoimmune haemolytic anaemia (AIHA)** | Pallor, jaundice, dark urine, fatigue. | Low Hb, elevated reticulocytes, positive DAT, raised bilirubin. | Start prednisolone 1mg/kg PO daily; avoid fludarabine. |
| **Immune thrombocytopenia (ITP)** | Bruising, petechiae, thrombocytopenia. | Low platelets, normal or high megakaryocytes (or none on film). | Steroids (prednisolone 1mg/kg) ± IVIG 1g/kg. |
| **Hypogammaglobulinemia with infections** | Recurrent sinopulmonary infections (pneumococcus, haemophilus). | Low IgG; recurrent cultures. | IVIG replacement if IgG <500 and >2 infections/year; prophylaxis with co-trimoxazole if low CD4? (rare) |
| **Pure red cell aplasia** | Severe anaemia with very low reticulocytes. | Bone marrow shows absent erythroid precursors. | Immunosuppression (cyclosporine, steroids). |
| **Leukostasis (rare in CLL)** | Dyspnoea, headache, blurred vision (WBC >400,000). | Very high WBC, retinal vein engorgement, respiratory distress. | Leucapheresis (urgent) + chemotherapy (e.g., bendamustine). |

**Late-presentation pattern (if untreated):** A patient with longstanding CLL who has been lost to follow-up presents with high fever, tender lymphadenopathy, leucopenia, and a growing mass – suspect **Richter transformation**. Immediate biopsy and PET scan are critical.

---
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonic 1: “CLL – Criteria for Treatment” (Rai stage I–IV with any of: )**
- **C** – Cytopenias (Hb <10, Plt <100)  
- **L** – Lymphocyte doubling time <6 months  
- **L** – Large nodes (symptomatic) or splenomegaly  
- → Treat!

**Mnemonic 2: “CLL on flow – Bright CD5, dim CD20”**
- CD5+ (bright), CD23+ (moderate), sIg (dim), CD20 (dim). Opposite: Mantle cell is CD20 bright.

**5 Clinical Pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**
1. **If an elderly patient has lymphocytosis and you see smudge cells on film, it’s CLL until proven otherwise.** No other cause produces smudge cells as a hallmark.
2. **CLL can cause immune cytopenias even with minimal marrow infiltrate.** Check DAT and platelet autoantibodies even if bone marrow is not packed.
3. **Arising diffuse large B-cell lymphoma in a CLL patient (Richter) often presents with a rapidly enlarging single node – not generalised lymphadenopathy.** Biopsy the most suspicious node.
4. **CLL is the great mimicker of other lymphomas with a leukemic phase.** Always get flow cytometry and FISH.
5. **Watch-and-wait is a real therapeutic choice.** Many patients are over-treated out of anxiety; the standard is to treat only when the patient becomes symptomatic or develops progressive cytopenias.

**Common mistakes to avoid:**
- Don’t call it “leukaemia” to a patient without explaining that it’s a chronic, indolent condition often requiring no immediate treatment.
- Don’t use fludarabine in a patient with AIHA – it can precipitate fatal haemolysis.
- Don’t give live vaccines (yellow fever, MMR, varicella).
- Don’t ignore del17p – it changes the therapeutic approach entirely.

---
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
| Parameter | Threshold | Action |
|-----------|-----------|--------|
| **NEWS2** | ≥5 (or ↓ in any component) | Call medical team; assess for infection, bleeding, respiratory failure. |
| **Lactate** | >4 mmol/L | Suspect sepsis or Richter transformation; escalate to ICU. |
| **GCS** | <15 or ↓ by 2 points | Consider intracranial haemorrhage (ITP), hyperviscosity, or CNS involvement (rare). |
| **Platelets** | <20,000/µL (or <10,000 if stable CLL) | Transfuse platelets; assess for ITP vs marrow failure. |
| **Haemoglobin** | <70 g/L with signs of decompensation | Transfuse (use irradiated blood products in CLL). |
| **Raised LDH + fever + new node** | Any | Suspect Richter – biopsy urgently; discuss with haematology consultant. |
| **WBC >400,000/µL** | Any | Leucapheresis; call haematology registrar immediately. |
| **Atrial fibrillation on ibrutinib** | New-onset AF with haemodynamic instability | Start anticoagulation (apixaban preferred); reduce ibrutinib dose or hold. |
| **Tumour lysis syndrome (venetoclax)** | Uric acid >8 mg/dL, K+ >6, PO4 high | IV fluids, rasburicase, ICU admission for cardiac monitoring. |

**When to call senior (haematology consultant):**
- Any suspicion of Richter transformation.
- First-line treatment failure (no response after 2 cycles).
- Del17p patient not responding to BTK inhibitor.
- Need for allogeneic transplant evaluation.
- Significant toxicity from therapy (e.g., grade 4 neutropenia, severe haemolysis).

rawMarkdown:
# Chronic Lymphocytic Leukaemia (CLL)
**One-liner:** A mature B-cell neoplasm of elderly causing clonal lymphocytosis, painless lymphadenopathy, and immune dysfunction; it evolves slowly but can turn aggressive.

---

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic vignette:**
A 72-year-old retired factory worker attends his GP for a routine blood test. He feels “a bit tired lately” but is otherwise well. Examination reveals rubbery, mobile lymph nodes in the neck and axillae, no fever. Full blood count shows a white cell count of 28,000/µL with >70% small lymphocytes. The lymphocytes look mature with scattered “smudge” cells on the film. He is diagnosed with CLL but has no anaemia, thrombocytopenia, or hepatosplenomegaly — Rai stage 0.

**Dissect the vignette (pattern clues):**
| Clue | What it tells you |
|------|-------------------|
| Age >60 | CLL is a disease of the elderly; median age 72. |
| Incidental finding | Most CLL is asymptomatic at diagnosis — a classic “lab surprise.” |
| Rubbery, non-tender nodes | CLL nodes are typically firm, mobile, painless; contrast with infection (tender) or lymphoma (larger, conglomerate). |
| Lymphocytosis >10,000/µL + mature small cells | Diagnostic threshold for CLL is B-cell count ≥5000/µL (often much higher). |
| Smudge cells (basket cells) | Fragile CLL lymphocytes crush on smear — almost pathognomonic. |
| No cytopenias | Early stage (Rai 0) — management is watch-and-wait. |

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
- **“Recurrent infections”** – A 70-year-old with multiple pneumonias, low immunoglobulins, but only mild lymphocytosis (10k). CLL can present with hypogammaglobulinemia as the dominant feature.
- **“Autoimmune haemolytic anaemia”** – A patient with jaundice, positive DAT, and a moderate lymphocytosis; AIHA can be the first manifestation of CLL, leading to a diagnostic detour.
- **“Painful node, fever, weight loss”** – This suggests Richter transformation (aggressive lymphoma arising from CLL) — a late event, but it can be the presenting scenario in a previously undiagnosed patient.

---

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Plain-language definition:**
CLL is a cancer of mature B-lymphocytes that accumulate in the blood, bone marrow, and lymphoid organs. These cells live too long (defective apoptosis) and crowd out normal haematopoiesis, but they function poorly – leading to both cytopenias and immunodeficiency.

**Why it matters clinically:**
- **Frequency:** CLL is the most common leukaemia in adults in Western countries (incidence ~5/100,000), with a long natural history that most clinicians will encounter.
- **Stakes:** Although often indolent (many die *with* it, not *of* it), it can transform into aggressive lymphoma (Richter) or cause life-threatening cytopenias and infections. “Watch and wait” is a real management strategy — doing nothing is often correct.
- **Common pitfalls:** Over-treating early-stage disease, missing Richter transformation, misinterpreting smudge cells, and forgetting CLL as a cause of autoimmune cytopenias.

---

### 3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind The Findings

**The story of development:**
CLL arises from a single B-cell clone that has undergone antigen activation (likely from a self- or microbial antigen) but fails to undergo apoptosis. The key abnormality is **overexpression of BCL2** (an anti-apoptotic protein), often due to loss of microRNA regulators (miR-15a/miR-16-1 on chromosome 13q14). This gives CLL cells a survival advantage, not a proliferation advantage — they accumulate slowly over years.

**Why each key sign occurs (mechanism → sign):**
- **Lymphocytosis:** The clone expands relentlessly, crowding the blood. The cells are CD5+, CD23+, surface Ig weak – this immunophenotype distinguishes CLL from mantle cell (CD5+, but CD23-) and other lymphomas.
- **Lymphadenopathy & splenomegaly:** CLL cells home to lymphoid follicles and interact with the microenvironment (nurse-like cells, T-cells) that provide survival signals. Nodes enlarge painlessly.
- **Hypogammaglobulinemia & repeated infections:** The malignant B-cells suppress normal B-cell development (via cytokines and competition) leading to low IgG, impaired antibody responses, and defective opsonisation – especially encapsulated bacteria (pneumococcus, haemophilus).
- **Cytopenias (anaemia, thrombocytopenia) – direct:** Bone marrow infiltration by CLL cells replaces normal haematopoietic tissue. This is a late sign (stage III/IV).
- **Cytopenias – immune-mediated:** CLL disrupts T-cell regulation, leading to autoantibodies against RBCs (AIHA – warm Coombs positive) or platelets (ITP). This can occur even with mild marrow involvement.
- **Smudge cells:** The CLL lymphocyte cell membrane is extremely fragile due to abnormal cytoskeleton – simply making the blood film crushes them. Not just an artefact; it reflects the defective biology.

**Connect to pattern recognition:**
The incidental lymphocytosis in our vignette is pure accumulation. The absence of cytopenias means the bone marrow reserve is still adequate. The smudge cells are your clue that these aren’t reactive lymphocytes (which are robust). The rubbery nodes are tactile evidence of clonal B-cell homing.

---

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Cause / Trigger | 1-line Stem Clue |
|----------|----------------|------------------|
| **Idiopathic** (vast majority) | Spontaneous clonal expansion; no known initiator. | An elderly patient with no toxin exposure, just “found” on routine labs. |
| **Familial / Genetic** | Rare; first-degree relative of CLL patient has 2-6× risk. Associated with certain HLA haplotypes. | Ask: “Anyone in the family had leukaemia or lymphoma?” |
| **Environmental** | Agent Orange (Vietnam vet), benzene, radiation – weak or inconsistent associations. | “Worked with solvents” or “service in Vietnam.” |
| **Precursor lesion** | Monoclonal B-cell lymphocytosis (MBL) – a pre-malignant state with <5000 B-cells/µL. | Incidental finding; progression to CLL at ~1–2% per year. |
| **Triggers for progression** | TET2, NOTCH1, or SF3B1 mutations; del17p (p53 loss); unmutated IGHV. | “Patient who was stable now has rapidly rising WCC, B symptoms, new cytopenias.” |

**Most common cause:** Idiopathic (no clear trigger). CLL is a disease of biological ageing of the immune system, not a reaction to a specific vaccine, virus, or toxin.

---

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria (IWCLL 2018):**
- **Clonal B-cell count ≥5000/µL** in peripheral blood for at least 3 months.
- If B-cells <5000/µL but with cytopenia or symptoms, bone marrow biopsy may be needed.

**Test-by-test approach:**

| Test | What it shows | Why you order it | How to interpret |
|------|---------------|-----------------|------------------|
| **FBC + blood film** | Lymphocytosis, smudge cells | First step – screens for CLL | Lymphocytes >5000/µL, mature, smudge cells present. If blasts or prolymphocytes >55%, think PLL. |
| **Immunophenotyping (flow cytometry)** | Surface markers: CD5+, CD23+, CD20 (dim), sIg (weak) | Confirms clonal B-cell and distinguishes from other lymphomas | **CD5+ and CD23+ is the hallmark.** Mantle cell: CD5+, CD23-. Splenic marginal zone: CD5-, CD23-. |
| **Cytogenetics (FISH)** | del13q, del11q, del17p, trisomy 12 | Prognostic stratification; guides therapy | **del17p** = p53 loss, poor response to chemo; fits for targeted therapy (BTK inhibitors). **del11q** = ATM loss, bulky disease, also poor. **Isolated del13q** = good prognosis. |
| **IGHV mutational status** | Mutated vs unmutated IGHV genes | Predicts time to progression | **Mutated** (~50%) → indolent course, median survival >20 years. **Unmutated** → aggressive, rapid progression, need early treatment. |
| **Serum immunoglobulins** | IgG, IgA, IgM | Baseline for infection risk | Low IgG → consider IVIG if recurrent infections. |
| **Direct antiglobulin test (DAT)** | Coombs test for AIHA | Check for autoimmune haemolysis | Positive → supports treatment with steroids; avoid fludarabine-based regimens (may exacerbate AIHA). |
| **Bone marrow biopsy** | Percentage of lymphocytes in marrow | Reserved for cytopenias of unclear cause, or before initiating therapy (document baseline) | >30% lymphocytes is typical; but not required for diagnosis if blood criteria met. |

**Single finding that confirms:** Flow cytometry showing a clonal B-cell population that coexpresses **CD5 and CD23** in the context of an absolute B-cell count ≥5000/µL.

**Single finding that rules out:** Absence of a clonal B-cell excess (B-cells <5000/µL) and no other clinical features – but watch for MBL.

---

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Philosophy:** CLL is often incurable with conventional therapies, but can be managed for years. Treatment is initiated **only for symptomatic or progressive disease** (Rai stage I–IV with B symptoms, symptomatic lymphadenopathy, severe cytopenias, rapid doubling time). Staging systems: Rai (0–IV) or Binet (A–C).

**Immediate (first hour / first visit):**
- If asymptomatic, **no intervention** – explain the concept of “watch and wait.” Reassure the patient that doing nothing is evidence-based.
- If symptomatic (e.g., painful nodes, night sweats, fatigue from anaemia) → assess urgent need for treatment, but don’t start chemotherapy in the first hour. Order confirmatory tests.
- Obtain blood for immunophenotyping, FISH, IGHV, LDH, creatinine, LFTs, serum immunoglobulins, DAT.
- **What NOT to do:** Do not start steroids or chemotherapy without a definitive diagnosis and staging.

**Ward / clinic (24–72 hours) – starting treatment (first-line):**
Choice depends on: fitness (age, comorbidities), IGHV status, del17p/del11q.

- **Fit patient, no del17p, mutated IGHV:**
  - *FCR* (fludarabine 25mg/m² IV day 1–3 + cyclophosphamide 250mg/m² IV day 1–3 + rituximab 375mg/m² IV day 1, cycle 28 days) x 6 cycles.
  - **Why:** Fludarabine-based chemoimmunotherapy is the historic standard; high CR rates in mutated IGHV.
  - **Pitfall:** Avoid fludarabine in patients with AIHA (may worsen haemolysis). Use bendamustine + rituximab (BR) instead.

- **Unfit patient, no del17p:**
  - *BR* (bendamustine 90mg/m² IV day 1–2 + rituximab 375mg/m² day 1, cycle 28 days) x 6 cycles.
  - **Why:** Less myelosuppressive than FCR, better tolerated in elderly.

- **Patient with del17p or high-risk (NOTCH1, TP53):**
  - *Ibrutinib* 420mg PO daily (BTK inhibitor) continuously until progression.
  - **Why:** Bypasses p53 pathway; excellent response even in TP53-mutated.
  - Alternative: *Venetoclax* (BCL2 inhibitor) + obinutuzumab – fixed duration (12 cycles) but requires TLS prophylaxis.

**Long-term:**
- Monitor with FBC every 3–6 months if on active therapy. Watch for drug toxicities: ibrutinib → atrial fibrillation, bleeding, HTN; venetoclax → TLS (monitor potassium, uric acid, phosphate; hydrate, give allopurinol).
- If disease progression on ibrutinib → switch to venetoclax (or vice versa). Consider allogeneic stem cell transplant for younger fit patients with refractory disease.
- **Supportive care:**
  - Vaccinate against encapsulated bacteria (pneumococcal, haemophilus, meningococcal) – **avoid live vaccines** (CLL is immunocompromised).
  - IVIG 400mg/kg every 3–4 weeks if IgG <500 mg/dL and recurrent infections.
  - Growth factors (G-CSF) for neutropenia.
- **What NOT to do:** Don’t use rituximab alone (poor activity). Don’t use maintenance chlorambucil (outdated). Don’t treat asymptomatic early-stage disease – it has no survival benefit.

---

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | CLL | Mantle Cell Lymphoma (MCL) | Hairy Cell Leukaemia (HCL) | Prolymphocytic Leukaemia (PLL) |
|---------|-----|----------------------------|----------------------------|--------------------------------|
| **Pain** | No pain from nodes | Nodes may be bulky but painless | Splenic pain (infarction) | Splenic pain, bone pain |
| **Exam** | Rubbery cervical/axillary nodes, mild splenomegaly | Rapidly growing nodes, often Waldeyer’s ring involvement | Macrosplenomegaly, pancytopenia; **no lymphadenopathy** | Massive splenomegaly, minimal nodes |
| **Blood film** | Small lymphocytes, smudge cells | Small lymphocytes with irregular nuclei; **no smudge cells** | Lymphocytes with cytoplasmic “hairs” (ruffled borders) | Large lymphocytes with prominent nucleoli; often >55% prolymphocytes |
| **Flow cytometry** | CD5+, CD23+, weak sIg | CD5+, CD23–, strong sIg (cyclin D1+) | CD5–, CD103+, CD25+ (tartrate-resistant acid phosphatase +) | CD5+ (in B-PLL) or CD5–; CD23–; strong sIg |
| **Key differentiator** | **CD5+ and CD23+** (classic) | **Cyclin D1 overexpression** (t(11;14)) | **Splenomegaly + pancytopenia + “hairy” cells** | **>55% prolymphocytes**; aggressive course |

---

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

| Complication | How it presents | Key signs | What to do |
|--------------|----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Richter transformation** (diffuse large B-cell lymphoma) | Rapidly enlarging node(s), fever, night sweats, weight loss, rising LDH. | PET-positive node (SUV >10); biopsy shows large B-cells. | Urgent haematology review; treat with R-CHOP or DA-EPOCH; consider transplant. |
| **Autoimmune haemolytic anaemia (AIHA)** | Pallor, jaundice, dark urine, fatigue. | Low Hb, elevated reticulocytes, positive DAT, raised bilirubin. | Start prednisolone 1mg/kg PO daily; avoid fludarabine. |
| **Immune thrombocytopenia (ITP)** | Bruising, petechiae, thrombocytopenia. | Low platelets, normal or high megakaryocytes (or none on film). | Steroids (prednisolone 1mg/kg) ± IVIG 1g/kg. |
| **Hypogammaglobulinemia with infections** | Recurrent sinopulmonary infections (pneumococcus, haemophilus). | Low IgG; recurrent cultures. | IVIG replacement if IgG <500 and >2 infections/year; prophylaxis with co-trimoxazole if low CD4? (rare) |
| **Pure red cell aplasia** | Severe anaemia with very low reticulocytes. | Bone marrow shows absent erythroid precursors. | Immunosuppression (cyclosporine, steroids). |
| **Leukostasis (rare in CLL)** | Dyspnoea, headache, blurred vision (WBC >400,000). | Very high WBC, retinal vein engorgement, respiratory distress. | Leucapheresis (urgent) + chemotherapy (e.g., bendamustine). |

**Late-presentation pattern (if untreated):** A patient with longstanding CLL who has been lost to follow-up presents with high fever, tender lymphadenopathy, leucopenia, and a growing mass – suspect **Richter transformation**. Immediate biopsy and PET scan are critical.

---

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonic 1: “CLL – Criteria for Treatment” (Rai stage I–IV with any of: )**
- **C** – Cytopenias (Hb <10, Plt <100)  
- **L** – Lymphocyte doubling time <6 months  
- **L** – Large nodes (symptomatic) or splenomegaly  
- → Treat!

**Mnemonic 2: “CLL on flow – Bright CD5, dim CD20”**
- CD5+ (bright), CD23+ (moderate), sIg (dim), CD20 (dim). Opposite: Mantle cell is CD20 bright.

**5 Clinical Pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**
1. **If an elderly patient has lymphocytosis and you see smudge cells on film, it’s CLL until proven otherwise.** No other cause produces smudge cells as a hallmark.
2. **CLL can cause immune cytopenias even with minimal marrow infiltrate.** Check DAT and platelet autoantibodies even if bone marrow is not packed.
3. **Arising diffuse large B-cell lymphoma in a CLL patient (Richter) often presents with a rapidly enlarging single node – not generalised lymphadenopathy.** Biopsy the most suspicious node.
4. **CLL is the great mimicker of other lymphomas with a leukemic phase.** Always get flow cytometry and FISH.
5. **Watch-and-wait is a real therapeutic choice.** Many patients are over-treated out of anxiety; the standard is to treat only when the patient becomes symptomatic or develops progressive cytopenias.

**Common mistakes to avoid:**
- Don’t call it “leukaemia” to a patient without explaining that it’s a chronic, indolent condition often requiring no immediate treatment.
- Don’t use fludarabine in a patient with AIHA – it can precipitate fatal haemolysis.
- Don’t give live vaccines (yellow fever, MMR, varicella).
- Don’t ignore del17p – it changes the therapeutic approach entirely.

---

### 10. Escalation Criteria

| Parameter | Threshold | Action |
|-----------|-----------|--------|
| **NEWS2** | ≥5 (or ↓ in any component) | Call medical team; assess for infection, bleeding, respiratory failure. |
| **Lactate** | >4 mmol/L | Suspect sepsis or Richter transformation; escalate to ICU. |
| **GCS** | <15 or ↓ by 2 points | Consider intracranial haemorrhage (ITP), hyperviscosity, or CNS involvement (rare). |
| **Platelets** | <20,000/µL (or <10,000 if stable CLL) | Transfuse platelets; assess for ITP vs marrow failure. |
| **Haemoglobin** | <70 g/L with signs of decompensation | Transfuse (use irradiated blood products in CLL). |
| **Raised LDH + fever + new node** | Any | Suspect Richter – biopsy urgently; discuss with haematology consultant. |
| **WBC >400,000/µL** | Any | Leucapheresis; call haematology registrar immediately. |
| **Atrial fibrillation on ibrutinib** | New-onset AF with haemodynamic instability | Start anticoagulation (apixaban preferred); reduce ibrutinib dose or hold. |
| **Tumour lysis syndrome (venetoclax)** | Uric acid >8 mg/dL, K+ >6, PO4 high | IV fluids, rasburicase, ICU admission for cardiac monitoring. |

**When to call senior (haematology consultant):**
- Any suspicion of Richter transformation.
- First-line treatment failure (no response after 2 cycles).
- Del17p patient not responding to BTK inhibitor.
- Need for allogeneic transplant evaluation.
- Significant toxicity from therapy (e.g., grade 4 neutropenia, severe haemolysis).
